story.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: dolphins swimming underwater
story.hook: Dolphins can call each other with signature whistles.
story.lead: Dolphins can call each other with signature whistles. Watch the group spacing, because dolphins use sound to keep track of individuals in open water. A signature whistle works like a personal label, helping the pod reconnect when visibility drops. Ocean social life is loud. Which sound should we decode next?
story.script: Dolphins can call each other with signature whistles. Watch the group spacing, because dolphins use sound to keep track of individuals in open water. story.description: A clip of cold-climate animals: penguin swimming underwater in an aquarium
story.hook: Penguins reveal the air bubbles first.
story.lead: Penguins reveal the air bubbles first. Watch the air bubbles first, because penguins trap air that can help with insulation and speed. When bubbles release, the water drag changes around the body. The smooth swim starts in the feather coat. Which polar trick next?.
story.script: Penguins reveal the air bubbles first. Watch the air bubbles first, because penguins trap air that can help with insulation and speed. When bubbles release, the water drag changes around the body. The smooth swim starts in the feather coat. Which polar trick next?.

story.description: A clip of mountains, glaciers, rock layers, erosion, or alpine landscapes: majestic snow capped mountain range aerial view
story.hook: Storm clouds show where a storm is feeding.
story.lead: Storm clouds show where a storm is feeding. Watch the spiral shape, because warm ocean air rises near the center and releases heat as clouds build. Rotation organizes that energy into bands. From above, the storm is not random; it is an engine. Which satellite clue next?

story.description: A clip of cold-climate animals: elephant seals relaxing on california beach
story.hook: Seals can follow wakes with their whiskers.
story.lead: Seals can follow wakes with their whiskers. Watch the whiskers near the water, because seals can sense tiny trails left by moving fish. Those vibrations help them hunt even when the water is dark or messy. The face becomes a current detector. Which ocean sense next?

story.hook: Dogs can follow a human point fast.

story.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: dolphins swimming in blue pool
story.hook: Dolphins can call each other with signature whistles.

story.description: A clip of dogs / puppies: golden retriever running in green meadow
story.hook: Dogs build a map with smell.
story.lead: Dogs build a map with smell. Watch the nose before the feet, because every sniff samples layers of scent. Dogs can learn who passed, where they moved, and what changed since the last check. The ground is basically a news feed. What should they sniff next?
